skill.name: doc-sync-tool
skill.description: Synchronizes AGENTS.md, CLAUDE.md, and GEMINI.md files across project folders, with manual and watch modes.
skill.summary: Keep AI agent instruction files consistent across a project.
skill.author: ZhanlinCui

security_audit.summary: Static analysis found many command, filesystem, network, obfuscation, and weak-crypto patterns. Review confirmed the runtime tool is a local documentation synchronizer, with no evidence of credential access, network exfiltration, prompt injection, or confirmed malicious intent. The real risk is broad recursive local file scanning and overwriting of agent instruction files, so publication should include a clear filesystem warning.

medium_findings.title: Recursive Documentation Overwrite Behavior
medium_findings.description: The sync script scans from the current working directory and copies the newest AGENTS.md, CLAUDE.md, or GEMINI.md content to peer files in each matched directory. This is expected functionality, but it can overwrite important agent instructions across a large tree if run from the wrong directory.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code directly uses process.cwd() or a wrapper-supplied directory and writes synchronized content with fs.writeFileSync. The behavior is documented and not malicious, but the overwrite impact is clear.

medium_findings.title: Persistent File Watcher Can Propagate Unintended Changes
medium_findings.description: The watch mode recursively monitors the configured root and triggers synchronization when target files change. A mistaken edit or unexpected watcher event can propagate content to related agent instruction files automatically.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The watcher delegates to the same sync routine after filesystem events. The risk depends on where users start the watcher and whether they expect automatic writes.

medium_findings.title: Helper Rename Scripts Modify Files Broadly
medium_findings.description: The rename helper scripts search from the current directory and rename matching documentation files. They use fixed commands and quoted paths, but they can still rename files across an unexpectedly broad workspace.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are hardcoded find operations, so command injection is unlikely. The confirmed concern is unintended local file renaming when launched from the wrong directory.

low_findings.title: Documentation Command Examples Are Mostly False Positives
low_findings.description: Most static command findings occur in markdown usage examples and shell snippets rather than hidden runtime behavior. They explain how to run Node, PM2, chmod, and Git hook commands for this local tool.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are prose and fenced command examples. They do not execute by themselves, although users should still review commands before running them.

low_findings.title: Network Findings Are Reference Links Only
low_findings.description: The only confirmed URL findings are documentation links to Node.js, PM2, and Git hook resources. No runtime code was found that sends data over the network.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Search found URLs only in markdown reference links. No fetch, HTTP client, webhook, or network transmission behavior was found in the executable files.

low_findings.title: Obfuscation And Weak Crypto Hits Appear Benign
low_findings.description: Hex escape findings point to ANSI color escape sequences, and weak-crypto hits appear to match normal words such as sync in descriptions and code. No cryptographic routine or encoded payload was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The suspicious hex values are terminal color codes, and package metadata describes a sync tool. There is no semantic evidence of encryption, evasion, or packed code.

dangerous_patterns.title: Synchronous Filesystem Reads And Writes
dangerous_patterns.description: The tool uses synchronous filesystem operations to read source instruction files and write target instruction files. This is core functionality but should be limited to trusted project directories.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The readFileSync and writeFileSync calls are direct and confirmed. Their targets are derived from discovered files under the scan root.

dangerous_patterns.title: Child Process Use For Local Find Commands
dangerous_patterns.description: The quick rename helper invokes fixed find commands through execSync. The command strings are static, but child process execution remains a risk factor in marketplace distribution.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The use of child_process and execSync is confirmed. Because the command strings are hardcoded, the risk is lower than user-controlled command execution.

dangerous_patterns.title: Recursive Watch Mode
dangerous_patterns.description: The watcher uses recursive filesystem monitoring and automatically calls the sync routine for matching file changes. This can create broad automated write behavior.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The recursive watch option and automatic sync callback are explicit in the code. The behavior is legitimate but has clear operational impact.

content.value_statement: Teams often maintain separate instruction files for Claude, Codex, and other agents. This skill keeps those files aligned with manual sync, watch mode, and helper scripts.
actual_capabilities: Scans a project tree for AGENTS.md, CLAUDE.md, and GEMINI.md files.
Creates missing peer instruction files in the same directory.
Copies the newest file content to older peer files.
Runs one-time synchronization through Node.js or shell wrappers.
Watches target files and synchronizes changes automatically.
Includes helper scripts for uppercasing legacy file names.
limitations: It overwrites target files based on modification time, not semantic review.
It only handles configured file names unless the code is edited.
Recursive watch behavior may vary by operating system and filesystem.
It does not provide built-in backups, conflict resolution, or dry-run output.

use_cases.title: Keep Agent Instructions Aligned
use_cases.description: Maintain consistent AGENTS.md, CLAUDE.md, and GEMINI.md guidance across shared project folders.

use_cases.title: Bootstrap New Project Docs
use_cases.description: Create one instruction file and generate the matching files for other AI coding tools.

use_cases.title: Automate Repository Maintenance
use_cases.description: Run synchronization before commits or builds so agent documentation stays current.

prompt_templates.title: Run A Manual Sync
prompt_templates.prompt: Use doc-sync-tool to synchronize agent instruction files in this repository. Show which files would be affected before running commands.

prompt_templates.title: Set Up Watch Mode
prompt_templates.prompt: Configure doc-sync-tool watch mode for my current project and explain how to stop it safely.

prompt_templates.title: Customize File Names
prompt_templates.prompt: Update doc-sync-tool so it also synchronizes our custom agent instruction file. Keep the existing exclusions.

prompt_templates.title: Audit Before Automation
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this repository for safe doc-sync-tool integration. Recommend a Git hook or CI step with rollback precautions.

output_examples.input: Synchronize a project that only has CLAUDE.md in one folder.
output_examples.output: The tool reports one matched folder, uses CLAUDE.md as the source, and creates AGENTS.md and GEMINI.md with matching content.

output_examples.input: Start watch mode and edit AGENTS.md.
output_examples.output: The watcher logs the changed file, rescans that directory, and updates CLAUDE.md and GEMINI.md when their content differs.

output_examples.input: Run the uppercase rename helper from a repository root.
output_examples.output: Matching legacy file names are renamed to uppercase variants, and the script prints the number of renamed files.

best_practices: Run it from a narrow project root and review Git changes after each sync.
Back up or commit existing instruction files before first use.
Use watch mode only when automatic overwrites are acceptable.
anti_patterns: Do not run it from a home directory or broad workspace root.
Do not use it when each AI tool needs different instructions.
Do not rely on modification time when conflicting edits need human review.

faq.question: Which files does this skill synchronize?
faq.answer: The current scripts target AGENTS.md, CLAUDE.md, and GEMINI.md.

faq.question: Does it send files to a network service?
faq.answer: No runtime network behavior was found. The tool works on local files.

faq.question: Can it overwrite existing content?
faq.answer: Yes. It copies the newest target file content to the other configured files.

faq.question: Can I change the target file names?
faq.answer: Yes. The target file list is defined in the CONFIG object in sync.js.

faq.question: Does watch mode run forever?
faq.answer: Yes. Watch mode keeps running until stopped, usually with Ctrl+C or a process manager.

faq.question: Is this safe for marketplace use?
faq.answer: It is publishable with a warning because it performs broad local filesystem writes.
